1: “On that day a fountain will be opened (tiqqāpēach maqqōr) for the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem to cleanse them from sin and impurity.” The opened fountain for purification is one of the great OT images of atonement. Ezekiel 36:25 (“I will sprinkle clean water on you”) and Psalms 51:7 (“wash me, and I will be whiter than snow”) share the purification motif. Revelation 22:1 opens the river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and the Lamb. John 19:34 notes that when the soldier pierced Jesus’ side, “blood and water came out” — a fountain-opening at the cross, connecting Zechariah 12:10 (piercing) and Zechariah 13:1 (cleansing fountain) as sequential events.
2: YHWH will remove the names of idols from the land and eliminate false prophets along with the spirit of uncleanness (ruach ha-tum’āh). Idols, false prophecy, and uncleanness form a triad of spiritual corruption. Acts 19:17-20 records idol-related books being burned in Ephesus at Paul’s proclamation — a partial fulfillment. The eschatological cleansing goes further: Revelation 21:27 says “nothing unclean will ever enter” the New Jerusalem. The Spirit of holiness (Romans 1:4: pneuma hagiōsynēs) counteracts the ruach ha-tum’āh definitively in Christ.
3: False prophets will be killed by their own parents for lying in YHWH’s name. The severity of the sanction (Deuteronomy 13:6-11 authorizes family members to execute false prophets) signals the absolute standard of prophetic truth. This is not an endorsement of the deathpenalty per se but a calibration: how seriously does YHWH take speaking falsely in his name? Jesus warns that false prophets who say “Lord, Lord” and perform signs will be rejected: “I never knew you” (Matthew 7:22-23). The false-prophet problem Zechariah addresses finds its NT resolution in Jesus as the final and true prophet (Deuteronomy 18:18; Acts 3:22-23).
4: False prophets will be ashamed of their prophetic visions and will no longer wear the hairy cloak (aderet se’ar) — the mark of a prophet (2 Kings 1:8: Elijah; Matthew 3:4: John the Baptist). Shame and self-concealment among false prophets in the day when true prophecy is vindicated. The hairy cloak appears in the NT most prominently on John the Baptist, who stands at the transitional moment: the last OT-style prophet pointing to the one who surpasses all prophets. After Christ, prophecy is both fulfilled (Matthew 5:17) and transformed (1 Corinthians 12:10; Acts 2:17-18).
5: The former prophet disavows his prophetic identity: “I am not a prophet. I am a farmer (ish mekhareh adamāh).” The denial echoes Peter’s denial (“I am not”, Matthew 26:70-72) and Amos’s own disclaimer (“I am neither a prophet nor a son of a prophet; I am a shepherd and a dresser of sycamore figs,” Amos 7:14). Amos’ self-distancing was authentic humility; the denial in Zechariah 13:5 is fearful self-protection. The contrast highlights what genuine prophetic calling looks like: not a professional identity to be defended, but a divine compulsion that carries cost.
6: “What are these wounds on your hands (makkat bein yadekha, wounds between your hands)?” The former prophet deflects: “Wounds I received in the house of my friends.” The wounds were likely cult-inflicted (1 Kings 18:28: Baal prophets cutting themselves) or from ecstatic practice — evidence of false-prophet activity now shamefully concealed. The verse has classically been applied to Christ’s nail wounds (the Passion typology is widely attested from early Christianity), though this was not the primary intention. Even as secondary typology it is significant: the one asked about wounds in his hands is the pierced one of Zechariah 12:10, whose wounds are acknowledged at his return (Revelation 1:7; John 20:27).
7: “Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who is my associate (’amîtî, my neighbor/close companion), declares the LORD of Hosts. Strike the shepherd so the sheep will be scattered.” This is the most explicit NT-cited verse in Zechariah 11-13: Jesus quotes it directly at Gethsemane (Matthew 26:31; Mark 14:27: “You will all fall away because of me tonight, for it is written, ‘I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.’”). The shepherd is identified as YHWH’s ’amît — uniquely close associate, near-kinsman — pointing toward the incarnate Son’s unique relation to the Father. The scattering of the disciples at the arrest fulfills this precisely (John 16:32).
8: “Throughout the whole land... two thirds shall be cut off and die, but one third will be left.” The remnant-theology of two-thirds cut off and one-third surviving echoes Ezekiel 5:2, 12 (the thirds of Jerusalem’s population). The proportion is a formal expression of judgment-with-remnant, not a precise numerical prediction. Rom 11’s argument about Israel’s partial hardening (“a partial hardening has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in, and in this way all Israel will be saved,” Zechariah 13:25-26) uses a similar logic: remnant election and eschatological restoration.
9: “I will put that third through the fire and refine them as silver is refined, and test them as gold is tested.” Refining through fire — the Malachi 3:3 imagery of the refiner’s fire purifying the Levites — appears here as the destiny of the surviving remnant. Then the covenant renewal formula: “They will call on my name, and I will answer them. I will say, ‘They are my people,’ and they will say, ‘The LORD is my God.’” 1 Peter 1:6-7 applies this refining metaphor to Christian suffering: “the tested genuineness of your faith — more precious than gold that perishes though it is tested by fire.” The covenant formula of Zechariah 13:9b is the goal of all refinement: restored mutual belonging between YHWH and his people.
